content: Dementia is a syndrome of progressive cognitive decline interfering with daily functioning, affecting ~10% of those >65 years, ~30% >85 years. Major types: Alzheimer's disease (60-80%, memory-predominant early, amyloid/tau pathology), vascular dementia (20%, stepwise decline, executive dysfunction prominent, cerebrovascular disease on imaging), Lewy body dementia (LBD, 5-10%, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, fluctuating cognition, REM sleep behavior disorder, alpha-synuclein pathology), frontotemporal dementia (FTD, 5-10%, behavioral variant—disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy; or primary progressive aphasia—language predominant; tau or TDP-43 pathology). Mixed dementia (AD + vascular) common in elderly. Reversible causes ('treatable dementias'): normal pressure hydrocephalus, B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, depression ('pseudodementia'), medication effects (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines), sleep apnea, neurosyphilis, HIV.
